text: Silver-copper (AgCu) bimetallic catalysts are frontrunners for the electrochemical carbon dioxide reduction reaction (CO2RR), offering a viable pathway toward achieving carbon neutrality. Despite the proliferation of AgCu catalyst designs, the fundamental question of how these materials evolve under operating conditions remains largely unanswered. This lack of insight into their structural stability masks the nature of the true active sites and hinders rational catalyst development. In this work, we synthesized both intermixed and phase-separated AgCu nanoparticles on carbon paper electrodes to systematically track their morphological and chemical transformation during CO2RR. Using time-sequential electron microscopy and elemental mapping, we demonstrate that Cu atoms exhibit significant mobility under reaction conditions; they migrate to the catalyst surface, leach into the environment, and eventually re-agglomerate as distinct particles. Furthermore, we found that both intermixed and phase-separated precursors gravitate toward a common thermodynamic state characterized by distinct Ag-rich (Ag0.88Cu0.12) and Cu-rich (Ag0.05Cu0.95) grains. This persistent phase separation occurs across both the bulk and surface of the catalyst, emphasizing the critical role of Ag–Cu phase boundaries in governing CO2RR activity. Complementary operando high-energy-resolution X-ray absorption spectroscopy further identifies metallic Cu as the primary catalytically active species. By mapping these dynamic changes, this study provides a comprehensive framework for understanding the chemical and structural life cycle of bimetallic catalysts during electrolysis.
